title:
After PR #47 merged, scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh PASSES against ams.truthkit.ai (8 bytes received in 25s, first 200 bytes := ': ping

') — but the wrapper still emits zero bytes during the first 15 seconds because the heartbeat is armed via setTimeout and the FIRST heartbeat does not fire until +15s, after which `: ping

` arrives
content: Empirically verified: `AMS_URL=https://ams.truthkit.ai bash scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh` returns "VERDICT: PASS" with first bytes received only when the heartbeat callback at +15s fires. The validator's 25s budget is intentionally generous (above the 15s heartbeat interval), so this is not a regression in the validator but a structural property of the code: the wrapper writes nothing eagerly, the heartbeat timer arms with a 15s delay, and the pump's `reader.read()` blocks indefinitely on an idle session. WebKit's streaming-fetch watchdog on iOS, per the operator's screenshot, fires faster than 15s in real-world cellular conditions — meaning #47 plausibly still leaves the iPhone "Load failed" path live.

title: Cursor Bugbot found 5 issues across the lifetime of PR #47, all of which derive from a single root: the wrapper has two concurrent contexts (the pump async function and the setTimeout callback) writing to the same TransformStream writer, with shared mutable state (`closed`, `heartbeat` timer handle) coordinating between them
content: Bugbot finding sequence: (1) network-tracking broken when proxy redirects browser to localhost; (2) heartbeat interval leaks when downstream disconnects mid-pump; (3) proxy hardcodes HTTPS; (4) heartbeat can corrupt SSE events split across chunks; (5) re-arm race orphans duplicate timeout chains. (1) and (3) are validator-side, fixed by #47's patches. (2), (4), (5) are wrapper-side, all rooted in shared-writer-across-contexts. #47 plugged each individually with `armHeartbeat` + `cleanup` + `clearTimeout`-before-re-assign — three additive patches across three commits. The mitigations are correct but the structure invites the next Bugbot finding because the surface area is the structure itself, not any single bug.

title: When a code surface accumulates more than two patches from the same automated reviewer addressing the same root cause, the right move is fresh iteration on the surface — not a fourth patch — because the reviewer is correctly identifying that the structure is the bug and each patch only narrows the failure rather than removing the failure mode
content: Pattern instance: PR #47's `wrapWithSseHeartbeat` accumulated three Bugbot-driven worker-side patches (commits 2e3d9c2, ed991f4, 404dbe0 — "track proxy host and prevent SSE heartbeat leak", "re-arm SSE heartbeat after each pump write", "clear pending heartbeat timeout in armHeartbeat to prevent duplicate chains") all addressing concurrency hazards in a 30-line function. Each patch added more shared mutable state (`closed`, `heartbeat`, `armHeartbeat`, `cleanup`) and more cross-context coordination. The fresh-iteration cost was small: one ReadableStream constructor with a single sequential loop racing one fresh timeout per iteration against `reader.read()`. ~30 lines of mechanism replacing ~70, all five Bugbot findings impossible by construction (one writer, one timer per iteration, no shared state, cancel handler propagates to upstream). Operator's reaction surfaced the steering pattern explicitly: "Bug bot keeps finding bugs in your code bro" — the Nth-patch frustration is the signal that the structure is the bug. This learning is co-occurring with the existing fresh-iteration-over-steering insight from journal/2026-05-05-curl-claim-done-incident.tsv (which named the same anti-pattern in the homepage mcpInitialize/mcpToolCall helper-function chain); this entry records the second instance of the same structural failure in 24 hours, confirming it is a repeating pattern worth promoting to canon as a constraint.

title: Replace `wrapWithSseHeartbeat` with a single-loop ReadableStream-based implementation that: (a) emits `:ok\n\n` immediately on response start (leading-byte flush — the actual user-visible improvement), (b) races one `reader.read()` against one fresh setTimeout per iteration (no shared timer state, no setInterval, no chained setTimeout, no `armHeartbeat`), (c) propagates downstream cancel() to upstream via the hoisted reader (no leak when downstream disconnects)
content: The new implementation is in worker/src/mcp.ts wrapWithSseHeartbeat, ~30 lines of mechanism. Structure: ReadableStream constructor with `start(controller)` containing a single async loop; each iteration creates one timeout, races it against `reader.read()`, clears the timeout the moment read resolves, then either enqueues the upstream chunk OR (on heartbeat) enqueues `:keepalive\n\n` and continues. The `reader` reference is hoisted to the wrapper's scope so the `cancel(reason)` handler can call `reader.cancel(reason)` — this is required because once `start` has acquired the reader, `upstream.cancel()` throws (locked-stream TypeError) which the previous draft swallowed in `.catch()`, silently breaking propagation to the SDK's inner WebSocket. Bug found and fixed during local proof-script testing before push. Confidence: established (4/4 local tests pass: leading-byte arrives at +0ms, real frames forward verbatim, 100 back-to-back chunks arrive in order with zero mid-event heartbeat injection, downstream cancel correctly triggers upstream cancel). Reversibility: two-way (revert worker/src/mcp.ts to restore #47's patched version). Disconfirmer: if iOS Safari STILL surfaces "Load failed" after this deploys, the necessary-cause hypothesis (zero-bytes-for-15s) is wrong; plan B is to drop the SSE leg from the homepage entirely and use ams_recv long-poll, already shipped as the documented degradation path.

title: Not adding a new validator script in this PR — scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh on main already covers the substrate property; tightening its budget from 25s to 2s to assert leading-byte flush is queued as a small follow-up, separate from the wrapper rewrite for review focus
content: The check-sse-heartbeat.sh script's 25s budget made sense when the contract was "heartbeat within 15s"; with the leading flush the new contract is "first byte within ~ms". Lowering the default `MAX_IDLE_S` from 25 to 2 would catch a future regression where the leading-byte flush gets removed (the exact iOS Safari failure mode this PR addresses). Deferring to keep this PR's diff laser-focused on the wrapper rewrite — the validator change is independently reviewable and one-line. The wrapper-rewrite proof is already in /home/claude/proof-wrapper-fresh.mjs (4 tests, all pass against the new structure).

title: PR open against main with one-file change (worker/src/mcp.ts: replace wrapWithSseHeartbeat) plus this journal entry; awaiting operator review and merge — Cloudflare Workers Build deploys main on merge, at which point check-sse-heartbeat.sh continues to PASS and (substrate-level) iOS Safari should now see the leading byte within ms instead of waiting 15s
content:
On merge: (1) Cloudflare Workers Build deploys main to ams.klappy.dev and ams.truthkit.ai. (2) Operator (or any party) re-runs `bash scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh` against either host; expected output is still "VERDICT: PASS" but with first bytes containing `:ok

` instead of `: ping

`, and arriving immediately rather than at +15s. (3) Operator opens ams.truthkit.ai/ on iPhone Safari, clicks Mint in the §A TinCan section, and confirms NO red "SSE error / Load failed" frame appears. (4) If iOS Safari STILL shows the failure, the necessary-cause hypothesis is wrong; revert to plan B (drop SSE leg from homepage, use ams_recv long-poll). (5) Follow-ups queued: (a) tighten scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh MAX_IDLE_S default from 25s to 2s to catch leading-byte regression; (b) canon-only PR adding fresh-iteration-over-steering as a tier-2 constraint, citing this PR + the May-5 curl-claim-done incident as the two co-occurring instances of the structural failure pattern; (c) upstream issue/PR against cloudflare/agents to add keepalive support to the SDK's GET handler at dist/mcp/index.js lines 224–302.

title: After PR #49 merged and CF Workers Build deployed main to both ams.klappy.dev and ams.truthkit.ai (Workers Builds check-run conclusion=success on commit 0baadda), GET /mcp now emits 5 body bytes within 110ms — exactly :ok\n\n — replacing the prior zero-bytes-for-15s state confirmed against the same hosts pre-merge
content:
Substrate verification, two probes: (1) scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh against both hosts returns "VERDICT: PASS" with first 200 bytes := ":ok

:keepalive

" and 17 bytes received in 25s (5 leading + 12 keepalive), versus pre-merge ": ping

" arriving only at +15s; (2) targeted curl --max-time 2 with -w "time_starttransfer" against ams.truthkit.ai shows first_byte=0.110s, size=5b, body=":ok

" verbatim. Network round-trip + TLS handshake account for the 110ms; the server-side flush is essentially instantaneous. Both deployments confirmed within the same 60-second window. Captured to /home/claude/post-merge-{truthkit,klappy}.txt at server_time 2026-05-05T16:57Z. Outcome at the substrate layer matches the prediction; iPhone-Safari outcome verification is on the operator (no device farm in this environment).

title: Wrapper-rewrite slice closed at the substrate layer; regression-prevention slice opened as PR #N (claude/sse-keepalive-regression-prevention-3e13) per operator's directive "make sure we prevent regression"
content: The regression-prevention PR ships three pieces in one diff: (a) tighten scripts/check-sse-heartbeat.sh MAX_IDLE_S default 25→2 so a future commit that removes the leading-byte flush demonstrably fails the check (the pre-merge zero-bytes state would now exit 1 instead of waiting for the +15s heartbeat); (b) productionize the local proof script as scripts/test-sse-wrapper-unit.mjs with the four assertions that proved the wrapper rewrite (leading byte at +0ms, frames forwarded verbatim, no mid-event injection across 100 back-to-back chunks, downstream cancel propagates to upstream cancel); (c) new .github/workflows/sse-keepalive.yml — the unit script runs on every PR touching worker/src/mcp.ts (catches structural regressions at code review time, no deploy needed), the curl probe runs on every push to main after CF deploy completes (catches deployed-state regressions immediately). The unit + e2e split mirrors the substrate-vs-outcome distinction the outcome-verification-via-runnable-artifact constraint already encodes for AMS.
